ai_summary.one_liner: LLM-as-a-Verifier turns scoring-token logit distributions into continuous trajectory scores and scales verification through granularity, repetition, and criteria decomposition, reaching state of the art across coding, robotics, and medical agent benchmarks without verifier training.
ai_summary.problem: Discrete LLM judges collapse uncertain scoring distributions into coarse tokens, causing ties and weak discrimination, while trained reward models often fail to generalize across domains and exhaustive candidate comparison is costly.
ai_summary.solution: Compute the expected value over score-token probabilities, average across repeated evaluations and decomposed criteria, convert continuous rewards into Bradley-Terry preferences, and rank candidates with an O(Nk) Probabilistic Pivot Tournament.
ai_summary.key_result: The framework reaches 86.5% on Terminal-Bench V2, 78.2% on SWE-Bench Verified, 87.4% on RoboRewardBench, and 73.3% on MedAgentBench; it also yields VOC 0.966 on robot trajectories and improves LIBERO RL sample efficiency by about 1.8x.
limitations: Direct continuous scoring requires access to scoring-token log probabilities, which several closed frontier APIs do not expose.
Criteria are manually designed and repeated evaluations allocate compute uniformly rather than according to uncertainty.
Dense-reward experiments are limited to single-turn settings rather than long-horizon multi-turn reinforcement learning.
Repeated sampling reduces variance but cannot remove correlated verifier bias or systematic model-family blind spots.

research_gaps.question: How can continuous verification retain its calibration when frontier model APIs expose reasoning and sampled tokens but not scoring-token log probabilities?
Can evaluation criteria and repeated-compute budgets be generated and allocated adaptively from the verifier's own uncertainty instead of being hand-designed and uniform?
Does dense verifier feedback remain stable for multi-turn RL and long-horizon agent trajectories where early scoring errors alter all later states?
How robust is probabilistic verification to correlated bias, adversarial trajectories, and reward hacking when generation and verification models share training data or model families?

future_directions: Learn or dynamically generate domain-specific criteria rather than relying on manually authored rubrics.
Allocate repeated evaluations and pivots adaptively using estimated verifier uncertainty.
Develop calibrated continuous-scoring interfaces for closed models without requiring a second open verifier.
Extend dense verification rewards from single-turn experiments to multi-turn, long-horizon reinforcement learning.
Measure and mitigate correlated verifier bias, adversarial gaming, and cross-model-family calibration drift.

relations.note: Both address scalable oversight of increasingly capable agent systems; this paper supplies a general-purpose fine-grained verifier rather than an automated research organization.
Both treat test-time computation over multiple candidate agent behaviors as a scaling axis, with verification and orchestration as complementary selection mechanisms.
ENPIRE depends on automatic verification in physical policy-improvement loops, while LLM-as-a-Verifier provides a transferable continuous progress and reward signal.
Both rank candidate agent outputs through tournaments; the AI co-scientist uses Elo debate while this work uses probabilistic pairwise scores and pivots.
